subject: Mathematics

unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 7! In this unit you will explore two of the most useful ideas in all of measurement: area and time. These are not dry, abstract topics that live only inside a textbook. They are the practical mathematics you already use, often without noticing. When Amina helps her mother work out how much cloth is needed to cover a market stall in Hargeisa, she is thinking about area. When Yusuf plans how long it will take to walk to the masjid and still reach it before Maghrib, he is working with time.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, activities and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured from the Cambridge Primary Mathematics 0096 — Stage 6 workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: Explain what area is and why it is measured in square units.
Calculate the area of a rectangle using Area = Length × Width.
Calculate the area of a square using Area = Side × Side.
Calculate the area of a triangle using Area = (Base × Height) ÷ 2, and explain why we divide by 2.
Find a missing length when you already know the area of a rectangle or triangle.
Break a composite shape into rectangles and triangles to find its total area.
Choose sensible units of area (mm², cm², m², km²) and convert between them.
Convert between decimal hours (like 2.5 hours) and hours and minutes (like 2 hours 30 minutes).
Add and subtract time intervals and find how long an activity lasts.
Solve real-world problems involving area and time, showing your working clearly.

concepts.title: What Area Really Means
concepts.explanation: Area is the amount of flat space inside a two-dimensional shape. Think of area as the amount of material you would need to cover a surface completely. When Halima spreads butter across a piece of anjero, the area tells her how much butter is needed. When a painter coats a wall in Nairobi, the area tells him how much paint to buy. Area is about *covering*, and covering is something you can picture. Because a flat shape has two dimensions, length and width, area is always measured in square units. Imagine a pile of…
concepts.example: The small raised '2' in a unit like cm² or m² is called a superscript, and it reminds us that area involves two dimensions multiplied together. It is a signal, not a decoration. Whenever you write an area, you must…

concepts.title: Area of Rectangles and Squares
concepts.explanation: A rectangle is one of the most common shapes we ever need to measure. Rooms, fields, books, screens, doors, prayer mats and market stalls are all rectangles. The rule for the area of a rectangle is short and powerful: ⭐ Rectangle Area Formula Area = Length × Width Multiply the two side lengths together, then write the square unit.
concepts.example: Why does multiplying work? Imagine filling the rectangle with 1 cm² tiles. Each row contains as many tiles as the width, and there are as many rows as the length. So the total number of tiles is length rows of width…

concepts.title: Area of Triangles
concepts.explanation: Triangles are everywhere: in roof shapes, road signs, sails, flags and bracing struts. To find their area, you need one beautiful idea that connects triangles back to rectangles you already understand. The key relationship: any triangle has exactly half the area of a rectangle with the same base and height. Picture a rectangle, then draw one diagonal line from a corner to the opposite corner. That diagonal splits the rectangle into two identical triangles. Since the two triangles together make the whole rectangle,…
concepts.example: ⭐ Triangle Area Formula Area = (Base × Height) ÷ 2 This is the same as one half of Base × Height.

concepts.title: Composite Shapes
concepts.explanation: Many real shapes are not simple rectangles or triangles. A house outline might be a rectangle with a triangular roof on top. A garden might be a large rectangle with a triangular flower bed cut from one corner. We call these composite shapes, because they are composed of simpler pieces joined together. The trick is always the same: break the shape into rectangles and triangles, find each area separately, then add the parts (if pieces are joined) or subtract (if a piece is removed). Example (adding): A shape is…
concepts.example: Example (subtracting): A rectangular garden is 12 m long and 8 m wide, and a triangular flower bed with base 4 m and height 3 m sits in one corner. What is the area of the garden that is left over? First find the whole…

concepts.title: Units of Area and Converting Between Them
concepts.explanation: Different sizes of space call for different units of area. Very small areas, like the cross-section of a wire, are measured in square millimetres (mm²). Everyday objects like phone screens and book covers use square centimetres (cm²). Rooms, gardens and stalls use square metres (m²). Whole cities and regions use square kilometres (km²). Choosing a sensible unit keeps the numbers from becoming awkwardly huge or tiny. Converting area units needs one important warning. Because area has two dimensions, the conversion…
concepts.example: Here are the conversions you will use most. 1 cm² = 100 mm² (because 10 × 10). 1 m² = 10,000 cm² (because 100 × 100). 1 km² = 1,000,000 m² (because 1,000 × 1,000). To change a larger unit into a smaller one you…

concepts.title: Understanding Time and Its Units
concepts.explanation: Now we turn to time. Time is one of the most useful measurements in daily life, yet it behaves differently from most other measurements. Length, mass and money all follow the neat base-ten pattern, where ten of one unit makes the next. Time does not. There are 60 seconds in a minute, 60 minutes in an hour, and 24 hours in a day. These unusual bases come from ancient astronomy and Babylonian mathematics, and they are why time calculations need extra care. It helps to know the basic relationships by heart. 1 minute…
concepts.example: Time can be written in two different ways that mean the same thing. Mixed units look like '2 hours and 30 minutes' or '1 hour and 45 minutes'. Decimal hours look like '2.5 hours' or '1.75 hours'. Both describe the same…

methods.title: How to find the area of a triangle
methods.example: A prayer mat is 120 cm long and 60 cm wide. What is its area?
steps: Choose a base and note its length.
Find the height that is perpendicular (at a right angle) to that base.
Multiply base by height.
Divide the result by 2, then write the square unit.

methods.title: How to find a missing side or height
methods.example: A rectangular stall has an area of 24 m² and a length of 6 m. How wide is it?
steps: For a rectangle: missing side = area ÷ known side.
For a triangle: missing base or height = (2 × area) ÷ the known length.
Check by putting your answer back into the area formula.

methods.title: How to solve a composite shape
methods.example: A triangular flag has base 16 cm and height 9 cm. Find its area.
steps: Split the shape into rectangles and triangles.
Find the area of each simple piece separately.
Add joined pieces, or subtract a removed piece.
Write the total with its square unit.

methods.title: How to convert time
methods.example: A triangle has area 42 cm² and height 12 cm. What is its base?
steps: Decimal to mixed: keep whole hours, multiply the decimal by 60 for minutes.
Mixed to decimal: keep whole hours, divide the minutes by 60, then add.
To add times: add hours and minutes separately, then carry any 60 minutes into an hour.

methods.title: Composite shape
methods.example: A shape is a rectangle 6 cm by 4 cm with a triangle of base 6 cm and height 3 cm on top. Find the total area.
steps: Solution: Rectangle = 6 × 4 = 24 cm².
Triangle = (6 × 3) ÷ 2 = 9 cm².
Because the triangle sits on top, we add: 24 + 9 = 33 cm².

methods.title: Area unit conversion
steps: Solution: Since 1 m² = 10,000 cm², we multiply: 3 × 10,000 = 30,000 cm².
We multiplied because we are moving from a larger unit to a smaller one.
Check the area unit conversion result carefully.

workedExamples.title: Rectangle area
workedExamples.prompt: A prayer mat is 120 cm long and 60 cm wide. What is its area?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Area = length × width = 120 × 60 = 7,200 cm². Both measurements were already in centimetres, so no conversion was needed. The answer is written with the square unit cm².

workedExamples.title: Missing side of a rectangle
workedExamples.prompt: A rectangular stall has an area of 24 m² and a length of 6 m. How wide is it?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Width = area ÷ length = 24 ÷ 6 = 4 m. We divided because division undoes the multiplication that made the area. Check: 6 × 4 = 24 m², which matches.

workedExamples.title: Triangle area
workedExamples.prompt: A triangular flag has base 16 cm and height 9 cm. Find its area.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Area = (base × height) ÷ 2 = (16 × 9) ÷ 2 = 144 ÷ 2 = 72 cm². Remember to divide by 2, because a triangle is half of the matching rectangle.

workedExamples.title: Missing height of a triangle
workedExamples.prompt: A triangle has area 42 cm² and height 12 cm. What is its base?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Undo the formula: base = (2 × area) ÷ height = (2 × 42) ÷ 12 = 84 ÷ 12 = 7 cm. Doubling first cancels the 'divide by 2' in the formula.

workedExamples.title: Composite shape
workedExamples.prompt: A shape is a rectangle 6 cm by 4 cm with a triangle of base 6 cm and height 3 cm on top. Find the total area.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Rectangle = 6 × 4 = 24 cm². Triangle = (6 × 3) ÷ 2 = 9 cm². Because the triangle sits on top, we add: 24 + 9 = 33 cm².

workedExamples.title: Area unit conversion
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Since 1 m² = 10,000 cm², we multiply: 3 × 10,000 = 30,000 cm². We multiplied because we are moving from a larger unit to a smaller one.

workedExamples.title: Decimal hours to minutes
workedExamples.prompt: Convert 4.25 hours into hours and minutes.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Keep the 4 hours. The decimal part is 0.25, and 0.25 × 60 = 15 minutes. So 4.25 hours = 4 hours 15 minutes.

workedExamples.title: Hours and minutes to decimal
workedExamples.prompt: Convert 1 hour 45 minutes into decimal hours.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Keep the 1 hour. Divide the minutes by 60: 45 ÷ 60 = 0.75. Add: 1 + 0.75 = 1.75 hours.

workedExamples.title: A working-day problem
workedExamples.prompt: Hassan starts work at 9:15 and finishes at 4:30, with a 45-minute lunch break. How many hours did he work? Give your answer in decimal hours.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: From 9:15 to 4:30 (16:30) is 7 hours 15 minutes = 7.25 hours. Subtract the 0.75-hour lunch break: 7.25 − 0.75 = 6.5 hours of actual work.

workedExamples.title: Comparing two areas
workedExamples.prompt: Which is bigger: a rectangle 7 cm by 6 cm, or a triangle with base 14 cm and height 6 cm?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Rectangle = 7 × 6 = 42 cm². Triangle = (14 × 6) ÷ 2 = 84 ÷ 2 = 42 cm². They are equal, both 42 cm². Always calculate both before deciding.

workedExamples.title: Convert 5 hours 12 minutes into decimal hours
workedExamples.prompt: Convert 5 hours 12 minutes into decimal hours.

workedExamples.title: Practice 12
workedExamples.prompt: Convert 3 m² into square centimetres, and 5,000 cm² into square metres.

activities.title: Grid-Paper Area Laboratory
activities.materials: grid paper (1 cm squares), a ruler, a pencil, coloured pencils.
steps: Draw rectangles of 3×4, 5×6 and 2×8 on the grid. Count the squares inside each one.
Check that your count matches length × width every time.
Now draw a 6×4 rectangle and cut it in half with one diagonal line.
Count the squares in each triangle (count a half-square as 0.5). Confirm each triangle is half the rectangle.
This activity lets you *see* why the triangle formula divides by 2.

activities.title: Home Object Measure Hunt
activities.materials: a ruler or tape measure, paper, a pencil.
steps: Find five rectangular objects at home: a book, a table, a window, a door, a mat.
Measure the length and width of each, keeping to the same unit.
Calculate each area using Area = length × width, and write the square unit.
Make a neat table of object, length, width and area. Which object has the largest area?

activities.title: The Same-Area Challenge (Investigation)
steps: Draw as many different rectangles as you can that all have an area of 24 square units (for example 1×24, 2×12, 3×8, 4×6).
For each rectangle, also work out its perimeter (the distance around).
Notice how the area stays the same while the perimeter changes.
Which rectangle has the smallest perimeter? Write down what you discover about squares.

activities.title: Time-Conversion Card Game
activities.materials: paper cards you make yourself, a pencil.
steps: On some cards write decimal hours (like 2.5, 1.75, 3.25). On others write the matching mixed times (2 h 30 min, 1 h 45 min, 3 h 15 min).
Shuffle all the cards and lay them face down.
Take turns flipping two cards, trying to find a decimal and its matching mixed time.
If they match, keep the pair. The player with the most pairs wins.
This trains you to convert time both ways, quickly and confidently.

activities.title: Design a Garden (Family Task)
activities.materials: grid paper, a ruler, coloured pencils, and a helper.
steps: Design a rectangular garden with an area of exactly 48 square metres. Find at least three length-and-width pairs that work.
Inside your chosen garden, draw a triangular flower bed and calculate its area.
Subtract the flower bed from the whole garden to find the space left for vegetables.
Explain your plan to a family member and tell them each area you calculated.

activities.title: My Day in Hours (Time Budget)
activities.materials: paper, a pencil, and a clock.
steps: List your main activities for one day: sleep, school, meals, prayer, play, homework.
Write how long each one lasts in hours and minutes.
Convert each time into decimal hours by dividing the minutes by 60.
Add all your decimal hours together. Do they come close to 24? If not, find what you missed.

After your activities, talk or write about these questions: Which activity helped you understand area best? What was the trickiest part of converting time? What would you like more practice with? Share your answers with your teacher at your next session.

rules.title: Key idea 1
rules.text: A rectangle is one of the most common shapes we ever need to measure. Rooms, fields, books, screens, doors, prayer mats and market stalls are all rectangles. The rule for the area of a rectangle is short and powerful:

rules.title: Key idea 2
rules.text: Two words need care here. The base can be any side of the triangle you choose. The height is the perpendicular distance from that base straight up to the opposite corner, always measured at a right angle to the base. The height is not the slanted side; it is the straight-up distance. Depending on the triangle, the height might fall inside the triangle, along one side, or even outside it, but the rule never changes: height is measured perpendicular to the base.

rules.title: Key idea 3
rules.text: The rule behind every one of these is the same: minutes = decimal part × 60, and going the other way, decimal = minutes ÷ 60. If you ever forget a value, you can rebuild it in seconds. Try covering the table and rebuilding each row yourself; after a few tries the common ones will simply stick in your memory.

rules.title: Key idea 4
rules.text: Keep a small formula card nearby: rectangle area, triangle area, and the 60-minute time rule.

terms: The flat space inside a shape, in square units
The distance all the way around a shape
A chosen side of a triangle and the perpendicular distance to the far corner
A shape built from simpler shapes
Time as a decimal, like 2.5 hours
Time as hours and minutes, like 2 h 30 min
Change from one unit or format to another
commonMistakes: Area = space inside (square units); perimeter = distance around

realProblems.prompt: At the Hargeisa market, Farah covers a rectangular stall top 200 cm by 120 cm with cloth. What area of cloth does she need?
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

realProblems.prompt: A triangular section of a roof near Mombasa has base 8 m and height 5 m. What is its area?
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

realProblems.prompt: A trip has three parts: 2 hours 45 minutes driving, 1.5 hours resting, and 45 minutes walking. Find the total time in decimal hours.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

realProblems.prompt: A rectangular room is 4.5 m by 3.8 m. If flooring costs 22 shillings per square metre, what is the total cost?
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: A composite shape is a rectangle 6 cm by 4 cm with a triangle (base 6 cm, height 3 cm) on top. Find the total area.
A square of side 6 cm has a triangle (base 6 cm, height 4 cm) cut out of one corner. Find the remaining area.
A triangular plot has area 150 m² and base 20 m. Find its height.
You have 24 m of fencing for a rectangular garden. What dimensions give the largest area, and what is that area?
A project needs 12.5 hours of work. If you work 2 hours 15 minutes per day, how many days will it take?
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: A square 6 m by 6 m gives the maximum area of 36 m² (for a fixed perimeter, the square is largest).

questions.question: What does “Area” mean?
options: The flat space inside a shape, in square units
The distance all the way around a shape
A chosen side of a triangle and the perpendicular distance to the far corner
A shape built from simpler shapes
questions.answer: The flat space inside a shape, in square units
questions.hint: Use the Unit 7 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: Area means The flat space inside a shape, in square units.

games.title: Quick Match: Area
games.description: Practise area through four short challenges.

rounds.prompt: What does “Area” mean?
choices: The flat space inside a shape, in square units
The distance all the way around a shape
A chosen side of a triangle and the perpendicular distance to the far corner
A shape built from simpler shapes
rounds.answer: The flat space inside a shape, in square units
rounds.clue: Use the Unit 7 Math Words & Symbols reference.
rounds.explanation: Area means The flat space inside a shape, in square units.

rounds.prompt: What does “Composite shape” mean?
choices: A shape built from simpler shapes
Time as a decimal, like 2.5 hours
Time as hours and minutes, like 2 h 30 min
Change from one unit or format to another
rounds.answer: A shape built from simpler shapes
rounds.clue: Use the Unit 7 Math Words & Symbols reference.
rounds.explanation: Composite shape means A shape built from simpler shapes.

selfAssessment: I can explain what area is and why it is measured in square units.
I can calculate the area of a rectangle using Area = Length × Width.
I can calculate the area of a square using Area = Side × Side.
I can calculate the area of a triangle using Area = (Base × Height) ÷ 2, and explain why we divide by 2.
I can find a missing length when you already know the area of a rectangle or triangle.
I can break a composite shape into rectangles and triangles to find its total area.
I can choose sensible units of area (mm², cm², m², km²) and convert between them.
I can convert between decimal hours (like 2.5 hours) and hours and minutes (like 2 hours 30 minutes).
